data.title: Hydreight Technologies' Compliance Infrastructure As A Service Is A Terrific Idea And It's Catching On Fast
data.description:
We wrote several times about Hydreight (HYDTF) for our investment group and recently bought shares at $2.25 and added at $2.52. The main reasons are:

  • Management issued FY26 guidance of C$150M base case.
  • The company's VSDHOne, its white-label telehealth platform, achieved 11K licenses at the end of Q1/26. This is up from the 1100 licenses it achieved in mid-November 2025 and 2.5K licenses at year-end 2025 (greatly exceeding the FY25 goal of 1K licenses).

That 10x license growth in under five months, it will be clear that there is something monumental going on with this company, but what? How did they achieve such a spectacular, and quite frankly, quite scarcely believable increase in licenses?

It took us a while to understand it, and the simplest answer is that they bought them in bulk. Before we get into the details, first, a description of the platform.

VSDHOne

While the main Hydreight app is designed for individual nurses, VSDHOne is designed for businesses such as med-spas, wellness brands, and health clubs that want to launch their own direct-to-consumer (D2C) healthcare brand across all 50 states without having to build the infrastructure themselves.

Brands need 50 different legal entities to operate nationally, a massive network of doctors to sign off on scripts, and complex pharmacy integrations.

VSDHOne provides all of that instantly.

VSDHOne is a SaaS-based model, which differs from the nurse app:

  • Partners pay for state-specific licenses to use the platform.
  • Hydreight takes a cut of every product sold through the partner's site.

Management expects each mature license to eventually process between 5 and 20 orders per day, creating a massive recurring revenue stream. Hydreight began rolling out VSDHOne 2.0 in late 2025. This update focused on automation, speeding up the onboarding process, which is a major bottleneck, and offering partners the ability to pick and choose only the modules they need.

The License jump

From the April 2 virtual presentation:

On that webinar, the CEO specifically said that this was for its "third vertical," which is VSDHOne, so we assume the 3K or so nurse licenses come on top of that, but the growth is so huge that these are finer details.

By the end of 2025, the company had already 2.5K licenses, significantly overachieving the 1K goal for the year. However, of these 2.5K licenses, only about 400 accounted for the bulk of FY25 revenue.

That's impressive, because VSDHOne, while soft-launched at the end of FY24, only started to really come to life by the end of June 2025 because of the lengthy manual onboarding process.

What's more, management reported 1.3M orders placed, but noted that many are stuck in backlog, a 30-to-120-day queue for clinical and pharmacy review.

So if only 400 licenses can produce something like $34M in revenue and operating half a year at best, imagine what 11K licenses can produce during an entire year. Onboarding has been sped up with VSDHOne 2.0 as well, lessening the bottleneck.

But how did they get to these 11K licenses in such a short time? They did a $15M financing in January, 3.7M units at $4.05 (with one unit consisting of one share + one warrant exercisable at $5.27), and they introduced some very smart moves to speed things up:

  • Creating new exclusive products with Perfect Scripts
  • Automating onboarding with VSDHOne 2.0.
  • The Health Accelerator is helping customers move over to VDSHOne.

Proprietary product lines

Hinted at in the January IR presentation (see slide above, second column from the left) and described in the April 2, 2026, webinar, management explained that the $15M financing from January 2026 is being deployed directly into financing product lines at the pharmacy level, enabling the company to participate directly in the economics of the product itself.

Previously, Hydreight simply sent a prescription to a pharmacy and collected a small platform fee. In the new exclusive vertical model, Hydreight uses its capital to pay for the raw materials, the specific machinery, and the specialized labor required to produce a specific drug at scale. In exchange, they own the exclusive rights to sell that formulation.

Perfect Scripts

One might remember the company took a 5% stake (with an option to increase this to 40%) in Perfect Scripts, a 503B compounding pharmacy. As a side note, a 503B pharmacy is much better, and this is what reduced Hydreight's risk with respect to compounded GLP-1 meds:

  • 503A pharmacies are regulated by state boards.
  • 503B pharmacies are regulated directly by the FDA under Current Good Manufacturing Practices (cGMP)—the same rigorous standards followed by Big Pharma.

A compounding pharmacy is a service provider that makes what a doctor orders. By financing the line, Hydreight changes the relationship:

  • Hydreight uses funds to purchase API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients) in bulk and install dedicated clean room equipment within a partner pharmacy (like Perfect Scripts).
  • They work with chemists to create a unique version of a drug—for example, a specific concentration of a peptide mixed with a proprietary blend of vitamins or buffers.
  • Because Hydreight financed the production line, that pharmacy is legally or contractually barred from selling that specific Hydreight-branded formulation to anyone else.

This creates an exclusive vertical where Hydreight captures the technology fee, the medical consult fee, and the pharmacy margin.

Advantages:

  • This produces higher margins; by owning the line, Hydreight stops paying retail prices to the pharmacy. They pay cost plus for labor, which significantly increases their gross margins.
  • This offers regulatory protection; by financing unique, proprietary formulations (like the buccal system or vitamin-enhanced blends), Hydreight can also argue its products are clinically distinct from brand-name drugs, protecting them from essentially a copy enforcement.
  • It also creates high switching costs; once a patient is on a proprietary needle-free or multi-peptide stack that only Hydreight provides, it is very difficult for them to switch to a competitor who only offers standard, generic injections.

Insu Therapeutics

Significant additional exclusivity could come through acquiring a $300K stake in Insu Therapeutics:

Insu Therapeutics is developing a proprietary buccal delivery platform designed to enable certain , FDA approved peptide therapeutics to be absorbed through the oral mucosa rather than through injection or gastrointestinal oral delivery. Insu's development strategy focuses on reformulating already-approved peptide therapeutics and advancing them through the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's 505(b)(2) regulatory pathway, which allows companies to leverage existing safety and efficacy data for approved drugs rather than undertaking the traditional full Phase 1–3 drug discovery and development programs. Hydreight believes this approach may offer a more efficient development pathway compared to traditional drug discovery programs.

While not ready just yet, this Insu contraption creates exclusivity; anyone who is tired or afraid of needles and wants to use it for GLP-1 meds or peptides has to go through Hydreight.

Peptide revolution

And there is more to come: 14 out of 19 peptides were moved back to Category 1 by the FDA, effectively green-lighting their use in compounding and use with Insu's Buccal System. And 100+ additional peptides are undergoing clinical trials.

The $10M Health Accelerator Strategy

As part of the VSDHOne rollout, Hydreight launched an initiative to invest up to $10M in cash, services, and shares to support emerging healthcare brands. It offers a host of services to brands that join, like (from the linked PR):

  • Funding: A combination of services, cash, and stock to support expansion.
  • VSDHOne Platform Access: Leverage the full capabilities of the VSDHOne platform, including telehealth infrastructure, e-commerce solutions, and nationwide compliance.
  • Marketing Support: Legitscript Certifications and Expert guidance and resources to amplify brand reach and customer acquisition.
  • Onboarding and Integration: Seamless integration with the VSDHOne platform and pharmacy.
  • Medical Direction and Doctors Network: Access to a nationwide network of physicians, and Healthcare professionals for Medical Directions and Consults

Hydreight provides the capital (from the January $15M raise) and the tech to a brand that already has at least $750K in revenue. In exchange for this support, the brand must move its entire business to Hydreight.

Hydreight’s API onboards the brand’s order data, routes it through their 503B pharmacy Perfect Scripts, and uses their physician network to sign off. The patient never knows they've been migrated, but Hydreight now owns the medical record and the transaction fee.

This is how the company acquired licenses, medical records, and patient lists without actually buying entire companies. They are buying the exclusivity of the patient data flow.

Medical records are the ultimate sticky asset. Once a patient's history, lab results, and prescriptions are in the VSDHOne database, it is much easier for Hydreight to upsell them into other verticals (like moving from GLP-1s to HRT or IV therapy).

This is why the license count exploded. By financing the migration of just a few large D2C brands, Hydreight instantly onboarded thousands of patients.

VSDHOne 2.0xx

This 2.0 version of the platform automates much of the onboarding process, making it much faster and cheaper. In the 1.0 version, migrating a brand with 50K patients required a team of humans to manually map spreadsheets and a team of doctors to open 50K digital files manually. Automating these processes has several economic advantages:

  • Onboarding was a bottleneck, taking 30-120 days, creating a gap between orders placed and revenue appearing. Reducing this bottleneck speeds up cash conversion.
  • By removing part of the manual labor required, it will also boost margins.

We couldn't find concrete info on how or what is being automated, but certain solutions suggest themselves, like Unified Schema Mapping, which automatically upload patient records and recognize the right fields for data, or Algorithmic Clinical Triage, automating the medical re-verification (every added patient must be cleared by a Hydreight doctor).

Economics

After all this, it's interesting to know how much the platform charges:

  • An up-front license fee per state (the SaaS model). That's $2,499 per year for the first state and $99 per month for each additional state, as well as a one-time $299 set-up fee.
  • A part of each order (transaction fee), this is the scaling feature that increases with volume, producing expanding margins.

Valuation

FY26 guidance, from a February 2 PR:

Based on current internal planning assumptions, the Company expects fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $150 million, representing a conservative baseline outlook reflecting currently contracted demand and visible partner activity.

This is C$ by the way, it's a Canadian company, and we expect that they'll comfortably exceed that conservative baseline outlook as it's based on contracted demand and visible partner activity only. There will be more. The second part of the guidance is at least as interesting (our emphasis):

The Company’s fiscal 2026 guidance assumes no material increase in fixed operating costs relative to late-2025 levels. Hydreight has already established its national regulatory, pharmacy, and technology infrastructure, and management expects incremental revenue growth to be supported largely by this existing cost base. Based on current internal planning assumptions, and assuming fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $150 million, management expects the Company to generate an Adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 15–17%.

Taking the FY26 guidance base case of C$150M in revenue and a 15%-17% adjusted EBITDA margin, the valuation is still pretty reasonable. With 53.4M shares outstanding, and taking its Canadian share price (NURS.V) of C$4, we arrive at a market cap of C213.6M and an EV under C$200M, so the shares sell for roughly 1.3x EV/S and just over 8x EV/EBITDA.

Share price decline

One might wonder, after all this fantastic news, why the share price is so low, well below where it was a few months ago.

  • The January 2026 financing (3.7M units consisting of one share and half a warrant) at $4.05 per unit was the third financing in a year.
  • The gap between orders placed and revenue recognized (backlog), but automating will speed up onboarding with VDSHOne 2.0, although we don't know to what extent.
  • It's also likely that the shares were caught in the compounding crackdown by brand-name GLP-1 producers and the FDA. However, tightening compliance is actually a strong tailwind for the company, as it basically sells compliance to smaller companies for which it's too cumbersome and expensive.
  • After a strong run-up in the shares, some profit-taking was also likely.

Conclusion

  • Hydreight has greatly accelerated licenses for its VSDHOne platform to 11K+ with several smart moves:
  • 1) It is automating the onboarding with VSDHOne 2.0, which reduces the main bottleneck to growing revenues and increases margins as it needs less labor.
  • 2) Its Health Accelerator is providing cash and other assistance to D2C brands to convince them to switch, with low capital outlays. Hydreight doesn't have to acquire them. Hydreight has moved from being a tool for individual nurses to being a compliance utility for large brands.
  • Its small stake in the 503B compounding pharmacy Perfect Scripts enables it to create exclusive verticals with little capital, which not only increases licenses but also increases margins as it's capturing the technology fee, the medical consult fee, and the pharmacy margin.
  • All the while, it's gaining access to a huge amount of end-customer EHRs, creating upsell and cross-selling opportunities, and customer stickiness.
  • It's making small investments in innovative companies like Insu Therapeutics, multiplying the opportunities to create more exclusive verticals (in the case of Insu, exclusive delivery methods).
  • Insu's delivery method will become especially helpful for the coming peptide revolution, as peptides have to be injected, with Insu providing an alternative delivery method.
  • The FDA, by moving 14 out of 19 peptides back to category 1, is providing more opportunities for exclusive verticals. The GLP-1 craze was just the starting party for a much larger peptide revolution.
  • Hydreight has moved from being a product company to an infrastructure company. By controlling the pharmacy lines, the delivery technology (Insu), and the 50-state medical oversight, they have built a cyclical model where more licenses drive more demand, which justifies more capital deployment, leading to compounding revenue growth.
  • Hydreight is benefiting from the regulatory squeeze on compounds as pharmacies no longer want to work with thousands of small, unverified businesses. They are reaching out to Hydreight because it is a rare 50-state multidisciplinary ecosystem that can handle the entire compliance burden.
  • In FY25, most of the revenue came from just 400 licenses, and VSDHOne only started to generate revenue in late June 2025. It also keeps adding new categories. Imagine what a full year and 11K licenses and all categories can produce.
  • Given the license explosion and onboarding automation, the company will likely see continued triple-digit growth, margin expansion, and operating leverage. It has plenty of cash and is cash flow neutral.
  • For all the above, valuation (less than 1x EV/S and a little over 4x EV/EBITDA) seems way too low for us.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Progyny: Significant Non-Cash Compensation Limits Value Case
data.description:
Progyny (PGNY) is a pretty interesting prospect. Fertility aid markets are definitely growing on some secular factors, such as later age of childbearing in the West, even if fertility rates are overall falling, increasing the share of childbirths through IVF and other fertility assistance.

As we understand it, Progyny is a benefit management company, though with a specialty of doing so for IVF clinics in the US, though they have a more general benefits management business as well for around a third of their sales. They seem to add some value in terms of the results in their programmes, but we are turned off by their middleman status.

Moreover, while the forward PE appears to be under 10x, that's only when ignoring very significant stock based compensation. Outstanding dilution from RSUs and stock options is around 21%. Taking an FCFY approach to assessing value, we get an adjusted 10% FCFY, adjusted downwards from headline FCFY by the effective obligation from outstanding options and RSU, assuming maximum dilution. 10% FCFY is quite attractive, but there are other picks in the fertility landscape, maybe something like Organon (OGN), or in Australia picks like Monash IVF (though embattled by operational scandals) that trade at comparable FCFY.

We much prefer the direct providers of the actual service than a benefits management company. Indeed, they have lost a customer reflecting some of the challenges of being a middleman. We don't like middleman business models.

Briefly on results, then the valuation issue

The company has a nice capital return policy, having returned $160 million, or more than 10% in buyback yield, to shareholders. This could be positive assuming that the company is really that undervalued. We aren't sure it is.

The growth is reasonably good, including in Q4, which has a tough comp from the loss of an important client and their health insurance scheme. Q4 YoY growth was 7%. There was growth for the FY was 10%, with the deceleration reflecting that customer loss. The fertility benefits management segment, around two-thirds of sales, grew 11%.

Gross profit rose on operational efficiencies as well. But note that operating income actually declined. The issue with this company is that there are massive stock based compensation costs. Look at the massive effect on the reconciliation.

The company claims as much credit as possible for admittedly a difference in clinical outcomes between its members compared to the general population.

This leads to the most salient point from our analysis, which is that considerable ongoing and outstanding dilution for shareholders paints a different picture than the initially screaming bargain 9x PE, quoted here on Seeking Alpha, which is based on the adjusted data it would seem. Based on RSU data and stock options outstanding there is as much as 21% possible dilution, supposing all the RSUs get granted and options exercised.

The business is definitely very cash generative, where of course this sort of incentive-based compensation has no draw on operating cash. FCFY is in excess of 13.5% on the basis of current operating cash flows, and the business is growing.

Penalising the denominator in the FCFY calculation by assuming full dilution and a bloom in effective market cap, the adjusted FCFY is something around 11%, including the valuation effects from possibly full dilution of the current $1.4 billion market cap and $190 million FCF.

When you look at comps, Monash as a FCFY upward of 7% - though it's operationally embattled due to some bungling of embryos. Organon is really quite attractive at 9.43% and is being looked at apparently by strategic sponsors. While Organon is still in the Women's health market and not necessarily in clinics, it's still a pharma manufacturing play and more attractive in our view than Progyny in the US healthcare value chain.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is that 11% FCFY is never something to scoff at with an equity that is growing profits a little and has underlying sales growth. But when there are hundreds of equities in general across markets with FCFY easily in excess of that, we are inclined to prioritise other ideas over Progyny whose SBC makes them not quite as attractive as they seem in a part of the healthcare value chain that we struggle to view as especially value add compared to the direct provision of these expert services.

data.title: Rare Earths Americas Launches IPO On Increasing Industry Consolidation
data.description:
Rare Earths Americas Is A Highly Speculative IPO

Rare Earths Americas, Inc. (REA) has filed to raise exploration and development investment funding via an IPO of its common stock, per an S-1 registration statement.

REA is an exploration-stage firm seeking to develop its land interests for the provision of rare earth materials.

The company hasn’t generated any revenue yet, is still some distance from commercial operations, and has numerous risks for investors.

What Does REA Do?

REA is in the business of acquiring and developing land assets that are rich in rare earth minerals, which are classified as 15 minerals from the Lanthanide series and 2 chemically similar elements.

Rare earth minerals are not actually all that rare, but they are dispersed and therefore challenging to mine in an economically viable manner.

The firm is also seeking to mine magnetic materials.

The company's portfolio includes the three projects described below:

  • Shiloh Project - Georgia, U.S. - 1,927 acres

  • Alpha Project - Bahia, Brazil - High-performance, permanent magnet minerals

  • Constellation Project - Minas Gerais, Brazil - High-performance, permanent magnet minerals

The firm also has rights to a number of early-stage exploration projects in Goias, Brazil and the Shiloh Project area in the state of Georgia, U.S.

Management said its growth plans include advancing its project portfolio through acquisition and development, pursuing strategic partnerships and “developing a U.S.-aligned platform to strengthen critical mineral supply chains.”

The company is led by President and Chief Executive Officer, Donald Swartz, who has been with the firm since 2025 and previously was CEO of American Rare Earths Limited (ARRNF) and has held numerous senior roles in other mining and energy development companies in his career.

Investors have invested fair market value of $46.3 million in REA and include Kitabella Pty Ltd, ACN 664400382 PTY LTD and various individuals.

What is REA’s Market?

While the company operates in the rare earths category, it appears its primary market is to mine magnetic materials.

Magnetic materials are essential components in electric vehicle motors, actuators, sensors and powertrains, as well as robotics, defense and electronics more broadly.

The magnetic materials market was an estimated $33.8 billion in 2024 and was forecast to exceed $48.1 billion by 2030,

If accomplished, this growth rate would equate to a CAGR of 6.3% from 2025 to 2030.

The Asia Pacific region dominated the magnetic materials market in 2024, with a 66.3% market share.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth rate of the magnetic materials market worldwide from 2020 to 2030:

Also, with growing investment in the energy sector, the need for permanent magnets will be subject to further demand growth in the coming years.

Competitors in the magnetic material mining industry include:

  • MP Materials (MP)

  • USA Rare Earth (USAR) - Serra Verde Group

  • Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF)

  • American Rare Earths (ARRNF)

  • Others in China and Australia

Of particular note, USA Rare Earth just announced the acquisition of Serra Verde Group, Brazil’s only commercial-level miner of rare earths, in a $2.8 billion cash and stock deal.

Vertically-integrated USAR said it plans to continue its acquisition efforts and organic growth initiatives to mine and process rare earth products for U.S. consumption.

REA’s strengths are its exposure to heavy rare earths, projects in the U.S. and Brazil, and its positioning against Chinese rare earths supply reliance.

The firm’s weaknesses are that it is still at a pre-revenue stage of development, appears to have a lack of vertical integration and hasn’t disclosed any customers.

Opportunities for the company include strategic partnership potential, government financing, and pricing leverage for heavy rare earth elements.

Threats to the firm’s prospects include large, existing competitors who are integrated, price volatility, permitting delays and uncertainties in Brazil and the U.S., and dependence on external processing.

REA’s Recent Financial Results

The company hasn’t generated any revenue yet, so its financials show only losses and operating cash use, per the graphic below of the results from operations:

As of December 31, 2026, REA had approximately $22.8 million in cash and equivalents and $20.4 million in total liabilities, of which $12.8 million was debt.

REA’s IPO Plan

The company has filed to sell its common stock via an IPO, and used a figure of $75 million for the SEC review fee calculation.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the normal & customary 180-day period following the closing of the IPO transaction. There are no “leak-out” provisions for certain shareholders to sell before that time constraint.

The company plans to use the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

to fund the land acquisition and option payments, drilling, metallurgical test work, permitting and S-K 1300 technical report summary preparation at our Shiloh Project. We plan to prioritize advancement of our Shiloh Project;

to fund the exploration, evaluation, land consolidation, metallurgy, engineering and permitting studies at our Alpha Project;

to fund exploration, evaluation, land option payments, metallurgy, engineering and permitting studies at our Constellation Project; and

the remainder for evaluating our non-material exploration projects, including Homer and Liberty Peak, as well as for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Management’s online roadshow presentation of the IPO is not yet available for viewing.

The company stated it is not currently a party to any legal proceedings that it considers material.

Underwriters of the IPO were listed as Cantor and Stifel.

Rare Earths Americas Is At An early Stage Of Development

REA wants to raise public investment to further its acquisition and development plans for rare earth minerals and magnetic materials.

The firm likely got a boost with the previously mentioned announcement of USAR’s acquisition effort for Serra Verde in Brazil.

However, REA will now be faced with a larger and more integrated competitor, which could put pressure on it to integrate as well.

Alternatively, the company could become more valuable as other mining firms seek to acquire assets in the Western Hemisphere.

REA’s growth strategy involved derisking its existing portfolio through additional assessment efforts, focusing on two-country exposure between the U.S. and Brazil, and strategic partnership development.

The global market for magnetic materials and rare earths is large, and the U.S. has recently increased its interest in securing its supply of processed products to reduce or eliminate reliance on China-based supplies.

However, the company is still at a pre-revenue stage, so it faces financing risks and execution risks even as it continues to pursue portfolio alignment with major markets and customers.

REA is also a “smaller reporting company” and “emerging growth company”, which enables it to produce substantially less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of stocks have disappointed IPO investors in their post-IPO trading performance.

While REA’s industry focus is attractive, the IPO will be highly speculative, and investors will have to be comfortable with assuming a wide variety of execution, financing, geopolitical, market and company reporting risks.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined

data.title: Capital One: Series N Preferreds Look Attractive With A H2 2027/H1 2028 Horizon (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The price of Capital One (COF)'s Series N 4.25% fixed-rate preferred shares (COF.PR.N) is little changed so far in 2026, withstanding pressures from a small increase in 30-year treasury yields (US30Y) as well as a double-digit slump in COF common stock.

In my previous coverage of COF preferred shares I only rated the Series N preferreds a Hold, highlighting their lower yield relative to COF's other preferred series. While the Buy rating on the other four preferred series turned out to be premature, pricing on COF.PR.N is now quite attractive and in line with other COF preferreds, warranting an upgrade of the Series N preferreds to a Buy. The bullish investment thesis behind COF.PR.N can be summarized as:

  • COF preferred shares remain well-covered both from a net income (29.5x) and common equity market capitalization (23.6x) perspective.
  • The relative safety is further amplified by the sizable 35% book value discount offered by the Series N preferreds.
  • I estimate a circa 10% total return for COF.PR.N investors with a H2 2027/H1 2028 investment horizon, principally driven by current income complemented by capital gains.

Preferred Dividend Coverage

In Q1 2026 COF paid $73 million in preferred dividends. Relative to the $2.15 billion of net income before preferred distributions, we observe that preferred dividends enjoy a robust 29.5x coverage by net income. This high coverage is in line with COF's financial results track record, with preferred dividends well covered even during adverse financial conditions, such as the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020.

COF currently has $5.4 billion in cumulative preferred equity outstanding. Relative to the common equity market capitalization of circa $127 billion, we see that preferred equity is covered roughly 23.6x, again underscoring the relatively high safety of COF preferred shares.

To sum up, we see that COF's preferred shares enjoy robust coverage from both a current net income and common equity market capitalization perspective. As I will discuss in the next section, the relative safety of the Series N preferred shares is further increased by the book value discount they currently trade at.

The Series N Preferreds

The Series N 4.25% fixed-rate preferred shares were issued all the way back in 2021, a time characterized by notably lower interest rates. Case in point, in July 2021 U.S. 30-year yields were around 1.90%, roughly 3% below current levels. This is largely explained by the Fed being below its 3% neutral rate in 2021 (actually back then the neutral rate estimate was even lower, at 2.5%), as opposed to above it in 2026.

In any case, the Series N preferreds only traded around their $25/share book value in H2 2021, slumping to an all-time low of $13.67/share in October 2023 when the Fed funds rate stood at 5.33%. As I write this, the price stands at $16.27/share, representing a book value discount of almost 35% and a current dividend yield of 6.53%. As such, we see that the already high common equity market capitalization coverage is further amplified by the sizable book value discount of the Series N preferreds.

In my opinion the takeaway for preferred investors is that the decline in Series N preferreds is largely driven by more readily available fixed-income alternatives, rather than a fundamental deterioration in COF's operating performance. Looking at Fed funds futures prices, we see that the Fed is likely to remain on hold in 2026 before resuming rate cuts in 2027, potentially lowering the Fed funds rate to about 3.00-3.25%.

While the effect on long-term treasury yields (which ultimately drive pricing on the Series N preferreds) is admittedly uncertain, I do expect a 0.4-0.5% decline to ultimately materialize.

This should result in the Series N preferreds trading at a current dividend yield of about 6.05%, or a price of $17.56/share. This represents a circa 8% capital gain for investors on top of the already attractive 6.53% dividend yield. I think a realistic timeline for the realization of the capital gains is H2 2027/H1 2028, ultimately dependent on the pace of Fed policy normalization. In any case, I think an annualized return marginally above 10% is achievable, thus warranting a Buy rating on the Series N preferreds, up from my previous Hold rating.

Risks

I think that the principal risk in the investment case is an increase in long-term treasury yields. This may be driven by slower-than-expected Fed rate cuts, or actual Fed rate hikes if we see a prolonged increase in inflation. High budget deficits may also undermine confidence in the U.S.'s fiscal position, as evident from the projected increase in the debt/GDP ratio to 175% in 2056.

Macro considerations aside, changes to the regulatory environment (such as the potential introduction of a cap on credit card interest rates) may undermine COF's business model, impacting coverage of preferred distributions. Given the high coverage starting point however, I believe the bar for a meaningful impact on preferred share prices is rather high.

I should also note that during times of financial stress and recessions, spreads of corporate preferred shares relative to risk-free alternatives such as treasuries tend to increase. As such, investors in COF.PR.N should be comfortable with mark-to-market losses during such periods, when COF's operating performance will most likely deteriorate.

Conclusion

COF's Series N preferred shares have withstood downside pressure from marginally higher 30-year treasury yields, a slump in COF common stock, and a shift in market pricing for Fed rate cuts from 2026 to 2027.

I believe this is largely driven by an attractive risk-reward in COF.PR.N, with my estimates showing that the Series N preferreds could reasonably deliver a circa 10% annualized total return for investors with a H2 2027/H1 2028 investment horizon, principally driven by a well-covered 6.53% current dividend yield, complemented by capital gains which are admittedly dependent on a favorable move in long-term rates.

Given the discount to book value the preferreds trade at, as well as their high coverage from a net income and common equity market capitalization perspective, I believe that a rise in long-term treasury yields is a key risk in the investment case. A change in the regulatory landscape for credit card issuers may also derail gains for buyers of the series N preferreds.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: RTX: Disappointing Beat And Raise Provides Buy Opportunity
data.description:
RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) shares dropped 4.4% even though the company presented a beat-and-raise quarter. The stock is now down 8.6% since my prior report, underperforming the S&P 500’s gain and comes at a time when the market expectations on progress in a peace deal with Iran are dimming. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q1 2026 results and explain why I believe the stock remains a buy.

RTX Shows Strong Results

First quarter sales grew 10% organically to $22.1 billion, beating analyst estimates by $630 million. Adjusted EPS grew 21% to $1.78 with 14% growth in operating profits and a 70 basis point consolidated margin expansion. The commercial OEM market saw sales increase 6%, while defense grew 9% and commercial aftermarket sales grew 14%. Free cash flow during the quarter reached $1.3 billion, which was up from around $800 million a year ago. While there always is the possibility of timing of cash inflows and outflows affecting the in-quarter free cash flow, I do believe that the significant year-on-year growth in Q1, which is the weakest quarter for aerospace and defense companies, puts a strong marker. Backlog grew 25% to $271 billion.

Collins Aerospace Margins Remain Stable Due To Tariffs

Collins Aerospace grew adjusted sales by 5% to $7.6 billion. Adjusted profit increased 6% to $1.3 billion, with adjusted margins rising modestly from 17% to 17.1%. Commercial OEM sales were up 15% but came at an unfavorable margin mix, while defense sales were up 9% and commercial aftermarket sales were up 7%, driving some operating leverage. The margin improvement was not impressive, but we note that there was a 1.3 point margin pressure from tariffs.

Strong Growth For Pratt & Whitney

$8.2 billion. The commercial OEM sales were up 1%, largely reflecting challenges for Pratt & Whitney to increase engine deliveries. Commercial aftermarket sales rose 19% with defense sales rising 7%. Adjusted operating profit increased 21% to $711 million, driven by higher OEM volumes in commercial and defense markets, partially offset by higher SG&A and operating costs. Margins rose from 8% to 8.7%. We note that Geared Turbofan margins are still negative to the OEM business, but high-margin aftermarket sales are ramping up.

Raytheon Results Reflect Expanding Defense Budgets

Raytheon posted sales that increased by 10% to $6.95 billion on higher volumes in the land and sea domains and higher naval munition volumes. Essentially, those are the same growth drivers as seen in the prior quarter. Adjusted profit grew 25% to $845 million driven by mix and volume combined with higher productivity. Margins rose from 10.7% to 12.2%. What we are currently seeing are productivity improvements, which were needed for Raytheon as the segment did not operate efficiently coming out of the pandemic. That efficiency improvement comes at a time when demand for defense equipment is improving. What we are seeing now in terms of growth is not so much the incremental increase in demand from the war in Iran but the longer-term production ramp that is primarily carried by adjustments following the war in Ukraine that sent defense budgets soaring.

RTX Beat And Raise Is Weaker Than It Looks

For the full year, RTX raised its revenue guidance by $0.5 billion to $92.5-$93.5 billion. It is always good to see a company raise the outlook. However, we note that analysts were already expecting $93.4 billion in sales prior to the increase from RTX. So, a more comfortable raise in the guidance would have been $93-$94 billion. Furthermore, we note that the raise is just 0.5%, and free cash flow guidance remained unchanged at $8.2-$8.75 billion. Earnings per share were increased by $0.10, or 1.5%. So, it is a beat and raise quarter for RTX, but not a big raise and not one that would impress analysts.

More interesting is what we see in the marketplace right now. RTX is increasing munition output. The company has already increased output by 40% and is working on a 50% expansion, paving the way for multi-year ramp-ups and elevated sales. However, we also note that supply chains are once again becoming the bottleneck, primarily in rare earth minerals, rocket motors and microelectronics. On the Geared Turbofan program, the number of airplanes on ground on AoG has decreased 15% sequentially while MRO output is increasing. So, the overhang is slowly but surely unwinding. In the commercial aerospace market, the Middle East war has not triggered any change in the outlook.

Putting it together, we see that demand is resilient, but it also leaves some downside as the economic impact of the war grows by the day. In defense, the growth is not driven by upside from the war in Iran but by existing growth in demand following the war in Ukraine showing how long it actually takes for demand to start translating to sales. At the same time, for the entire business the war may trigger inflation and higher input costs, which is a near-term risk without any tangible near-term upside in serviceable demand. What it essentially means is that the risk profile is elevating slightly.

On the topic of tariffs, RTX has now paid around $500 million in tariffs. The Supreme Court has ruled tariffs under IEEPA illegal, and tariff refunds have commenced, but RTX has not applied for a refund, and President Trump has said he will remember companies that don’t seek tariff refunds. In other words, he will presumably also not forget the companies that did apply, and that makes it less plausible that RTX will apply for refunds in the near future.

RTX Remains Attractive Despite Shallow Guidance Raise

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For RTX, I previously set the price target using the average of the peer group implied price target and the company median EV/EBITDA implied price target. I am now changing that to a 3-year EV/EBITDA driven price target for the company, and that would imply a $244.34 price target and 31% upside. This is a substantial lift from my prior price target of $227.44. That increase, however, is barely driven by any increase in estimates. For 2026, EBITDA estimates have remained unchanged, with a cumulative change in the estimates for 2026-2028 of 0.2%. For free cash flow, the estimates have changed roughly the same, and in both cases the improvement in estimates is backloaded.

We note that margins for RTX remain depressed with a dip this year and a gradual improvement in the years after. Free cash flow, however, is expected to rise substantially in the years ahead with a manageable capital intensity, further improvements in the net debt leverage and a cash pile that could grow to north of $15 billion by 2028. So, the company actually has a lot of space to increase shareholder returns.

Conclusion: RTX Remains A Buy But Beat And Raise Did Not Impress

I believe that RTX has a bright future ahead. A significant portion of growth seen today is strong aftermarket sales, defense growth following the defense budget expansion spree due to the war in Ukraine and the GTF impact unwinding. So, we are not seeing a lot of “new growth,” just increased demand propagating to the financial lines and pressures slowly but surely fading. While the Iran war could trigger additional defense spending, particularly in the Middle East, and drive demand for maintenance and spares, it should also be noted that cost inflation and macro growth could eventually become near-term contract risks for RTX. In the end, however, I do believe that the stock remains a buy. We do note that with markets near record highs and uncertainty about the situation in the Middle East increasing, there is downside risk, but I believe that RTX is attractive for long-term investors.

data.title: AMD's Rally Isn't Hype
data.description:
Introduction

It’s easy to dismiss this rally for Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) stock as just another momentum surge, but that overlooks what’s actually unfolding beneath the surface. With the stock trading at roughly $280, the company now has a market capitalization of around $450 billion. This represents a major repricing because the market is starting to see AMD not only as a leading CPU maker with a bit of AI side business but also as a potential second-tier platform for AI infrastructures altogether.

The reasons for such a sentiment? According to recent reports, OpenAI agreed to a 6GW multi-generation deal revolving around AMD’s MI450 GPUs, with the first gigawatt capacity expected to become live by 2026. Also, as if that wasn’t enough, Meta Platforms agreed to buy $60 billion worth of AMD chips during a five-year span, kicking off its AI expansion with a 1GW capacity based on AMD’s MI450 hardware.

From Competing to Scaling

The Data Center segment has become AMD’s bread-and-butter, contributing about 48% of total sales and having the most profitable margin structure. The company maintains gross margins at 55%-57%, while operating margins are close to 25%. It generates strong free cash flow and expanding operating income, although still below Nvidia’s profitability levels. However, Nvidia still leads on profitability, but I believe AMD can narrow that gap as AI mix continues to scale.

Additionally, the Client, Gaming and Embedded groups still contribute meaningful revenue to AMD's top line, yet they tend to be much more cyclical in nature and less strategic than Data Center. Personally, I see these three groups as having more of an operational importance today as a source of stability and free cash flows, with the true value being generated from AI and Data Center. That said, I do not believe that the markets will mind these three business groups having some bumps in the road as long as Data Center keeps on scaling.

It’s not just about expanding the customer base but about the significance and strategy behind these engagements. The important change here is that AMD doesn’t have to demonstrate demand anymore but can concentrate on its significance and sustainability. The collaboration with OpenAI involves the development of hardware-software integration in MI450 and subsequent platforms, whereas the decision of Meta Platforms to work with AMD reflects a conscious effort to avoid vendor lock-in.

On the other hand, Oracle’s plans to use 50,000 MI450 GPUs from Q3 2026 are an obvious indication of faith. It’s not an experiment but hyperscale infrastructure deployment that underlines the shift towards the role of a provider of an AI platform rather than just a chipmaker.

That's why I consider the recent rally of AMD reasonable. Now, we have confirmation that AMD is not trying to compete with Nvidia from a position of weakness. Moreover, TSMC's Q1 results are positive too, reporting $35.9 billion in revenue with a 66.2% margin. TSMC raised its outlook for 2026 to more than 30%, with capex guidance in the range from $52 to $56 billion. While I wouldn’t attribute AMD’s story solely to TSMC, its Q1 results (66.2% margin, >30% growth outlook) suggest supply constraints are not an immediate risk.

It’s Not the Quarter, It’s the Message That Moves the Stock

The quarter is important since the stock performed well recently, so the market's expectations increased, but what is truly essential? Can the management keep the second half focused on the upcoming AI ramp without sounding overly promotional? In February's earnings call, Lisa Su confirmed that Q1 data centers would continue growing, server CPU demand remained strong, and MI450 revenues would start in the third quarter with an acceleration expected in the fourth quarter. Besides, she clarified that AMD is assuming zero China revenue for the MI308 processor beyond $100 million of Q1 estimates.

That creates a peculiar situation. In case of good results and preservation of the second-half message, the stock price can continue growing. However, in the case of good but not great results and a small shift in the message related to the ramp, the share price can go south. Here, I would be a little bit more cautious than those investors who follow a straightforward momentum strategy. The business is growing indeed, but I believe that a lot of hype related to the second half of the year has already been priced in. Investors do not buy AMD because it's cheap compared to Nvidia anymore.

The reason for buying AMD is due to its ability to deliver a rack-scale AI platform based on MI450 with a significant inference relevance. This sounds like a much more attractive investment thesis, but the requirements for AMD are higher in comparison with previous expectations. Still, the probability that the stock will perform well before earning release is high.

Underpriced if the Story Shifts

AMD has a valuation of ~9.8x sales based on ~$46 billion in 2026 revenue, but unlike Nvidia, it operates at lower margins today (~55% gross vs. ~70%+). The valuation reflects expectations that AI-driven mix shift will expand profitability over time. So, AMD remains slightly richly valued until you project it out but well below Nvidia's multiple. Nvidia’s premium is also supported by its software ecosystem and execution track record, areas where AMD is still catching up.

The Street already expects AMD to achieve ~$68 billion in revenues in 2027, driving that multiple back down towards ~5x without even considering any valuation re-rating.

What the stock is actually playing out is the possibility that a sea-change in fundamentals could be underway. In case the MI450 successfully captures the demand of OpenAI, Meta, and Oracle and converts it to sustainable rack-scale revenue, AMD ceases to be merely a semiconductor company and turns into an infrastructure company. It will affect their bottom line, visibility, and ultimately valuation. Although AMD may no longer trade at a deep discount, I believe there is more upside ahead.

Momentum Is Strong

The current momentum behind AMD has been quite steady and long-lasting, and there does not appear to be any bubble involved. Over the past year, the company has returned an impressive 214%, far exceeding both its sector average and even its own historic average. Furthermore, the trend continues on all other time frames, with ~75% over the last 9 months and more positive returns over 3- and 6-month time periods.

The B+ momentum rating further validates my opinion. While the extent of the rally itself is impressive, what I find even more noteworthy about this stock is the stability and consistency of returns on different time frames, which often implies that the company has entered a re-rating phase. In addition, there have been several analysts' notes supporting this trend; the stock has no sell ratings but only strong buy recommendations.

However, such a degree of momentum can also turn into a self-sustaining phenomenon, but one should not underestimate the risks associated with it. As for now, the stock has already passed its initial stage and is in a more mature phase of momentum.

What Can Go Wrong

For me, the main risk does not relate to demand but to how well execution meets these expectations. These deals already reflect a significant share of expected upside and will already be "baked in" before the revenues are actually earned. What matters now is no longer customer acquisition, but converting these commitments into scalable, recurring revenue streams.

There are still many hurdles that MI450 needs to clear from development to manufacture, from assembly to optimization of software, and finally deployment at the rack level. Lisa Su has said that she is confident that the product launch will proceed as planned and that supply is no problem, which is certainly comforting to know. However, until we see successful execution through each of these layers, a certain amount of skepticism is probably warranted.

Conclusion

Heading into the first quarter earnings call, I will be closely watching three things, which are Data Center growth, MI450 ramp-up, and second quarter guidance, because these factors will influence the stock price.

I believe that AMD can break higher before earnings, but only with fresh validation. The stock may consolidate from here given the recent surge in price. Positive signs from capex spending by hyperscalers would see the stock slowly move higher, and any upside surprises on the back of artificial intelligence or share gains would be a sufficient catalyst to spark a breakout.

data.title: FMI Large Cap Equity Q1 2026 Review
data.description:
Investment Philosophy:

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Performance Quarter YTD 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception
Large Cap (Gross) -5.03% -5.03% 0.36% 9.67% 6.00% 9.77% 10.12%
Large Cap (net) -5.14% -5.14% -0.09% 9.17% 5.52% 9.21% 9.46%
S&P 500 Index -4.33% -4.33% 17.80% 18.32% 12.06% 14.16% 8.54%
iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF 2 1.97% 1.97% 15.57% 14.11% 9.21% 10.39% 7.53%

Inception: December 31, 2000

Performance is preliminary and subject to reconciliation.

Investment Process:

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 20-30 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.

Research Team PMC*
Jonathan T. Bloom, CFA ✓ ✓
John S. Brandser ✓
Robert M. Helf, CFA ✓
Benjamin D. Karek, CFA ✓ ✓
John P. O'Neill ✓
Jake E. Stroble, CFA ✓
Matthew T. Sullivan, CFA ✓ ✓
Jordan S. Teschendorf, CFA ✓ ✓
Dain C. Tofson, CFA ✓ ✓

*Portfolio Management Committee

Please note disclosure footnote on reverse side.

1 Estimated valuations are based on a representative account from the FMI Large Cap Equity Composite, and are weighted average calculations, not reweighted to exclude cash, and financial companies are excluded from the EV/EBITDA calculation. Valuations for the portfolio are modified based on criteria identified by FMI. For more detailed information regarding these valuations, please contact FMI.

2 Source: Bloomberg - returns do not reflect management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Performance is based on market price returns. Beginning 8/10/20, market price returns are calculated using closing price. Prior to 8/10/20, market price returns were calculated using midpoint bid/ask spread at 4:00 PM ET.

Top 10 – Portfolio Holdings:

Charles Schwab Corp. (SCHW) 7.6%
Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG) 6.1%
Ferguson Enterprises Inc. (FERG) 5.6%
Aramark (ARMK) 5.3%
Avery Dennison Corp. (AVY) 5.0%
Carrier Global Corp. (CARR) 4.6%
Becton Dickinson & Co. (BDX) 4.5%
Accenture PLC CI A (ACN) 4.5%
CDW Corp. (CDW) 4.1%
Allegion PLC (ALLE) 3.5%

Portfolio Characteristics: 1

P/E Ratio (trailing one year) 20.4x
FY1 P/E Ratio (forward) 15.3x
FY2 P/E Ratio (forward) 13.7x
P/S Ratio 2.3x
P/B Ratio 4.6x
EV/EBITDA Ratio 12.7x
Number of Holdings 26
Active Share 93.8%

Recent Purchase:

Sunbelt Rental Holdings Inc. Ticker: SUNB Added: March 2026

Sunbelt Rental (formerly known as Ashtead Group Plc) is the second largest equipment rental company in the U.S. In March, the company made their primary stock listing in the U.S., with a secondary listing in London, UK. The company's business is simple: they buy equipment, rent it out to customers, and then sell it after an average of 7 years. Their competitive advantage comes from purchasing scale and local density. They can provide higher service levels and deliver a broader range/depth of products at lower cost, allowing them to take share from peers while garnering higher margins. Currently they are experiencing a downturn in their non-residential construction exposed business. Despite this, we believe there are a number of structural changes to the industry that will make the current downturn much less severe. We initiated a 3% position in March 2026.

Recent Sale:

CSX Corp. Ticker: CSX Sold: February 2026

CSX is a leading freight railroad operating in the Eastern U.S. with a 20,000 route-mile rail network and access to over 70 ocean, river, and lake port terminals. CSX is headquartered in Jacksonville, FL and has 22,500 employees. The barriers to entry are extremely high as it's impossible to replicate a North American class 1 railroad. Additionally, if executed well, rails are advantaged to trucks due to lower operating costs and fuel efficiency. Our thesis was that the new CEO (Joe Hinrichs) was focused on improving the customer service experience to grow rail volumes vs. trucks. Unfortunately, our CSX thesis didn't work as the truck-to-rail conversion hasn't moved the needle on volume growth, and the CEO was fired. The rail industry has shifted its gaze from service-driven volume growth to consolidation. At the same time, the recent rise in the shares appears to reflect the operational improvements we expect over the next 18 months. As a result, we sold our 3.1% in February 2026.

Fiduciary Management, Inc. claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Fiduciary Management, Inc. has been independently verified for the periods 12/31/1993 - 12/31/2025. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. The Large Cap Equity Composite has had a performance examination for the periods 12/31/2000 - 12/31/2025. The verification and performance examination reports are available upon request.

FMI was founded in 1980 and is an independent investment counseling firm registered with the SEC and the State of Wisconsin. The firm manages over $11.1 billion in assets of pension and profit sharing trusts, mutual funds, Taft-Hartley funds, insurance company portfolios, endowments and personal trusts. The firm includes both institutional and mutual fund business. Although the firm has participated in wrap programs, it is a separate and distinct business, and is excluded from firm-wide assets.

The FMI Large Cap Equity Composite was created and incepted on 12/31/2000. These accounts primarily invest in medium to large capitalization US equities.

The FMI Large Cap Equity Composite reflects time-weighted and asset-weighted returns for all discretionary accounts with a market value greater than $500,000 as of month end beginning January 1, 2012. From December 31, 2000 thru September 30, 2002 all accounts included were managed for at least one quarter, from October 1, 2002 to present all accounts were managed for at least one month. All returns are calculated using United States Dollars and are based on monthly valuations using trade date accounting. All accounts in this composite are fee paying. Gross of fees returns are calculated gross of management fees, gross of custodial fees, gross of withholding taxes and net of transaction costs. Net of fees returns are calculated net of actual management fees and transaction costs and gross of custodial fees and withholding taxes. Dispersion is calculated using the equal weighted standard deviation of all accounts in the composite for the entire period. As of 12/31/2011, the trailing three year annualized ex-post standard deviation for the Composite and Benchmark are required to be stated per GIPS®. FMI uses gross returns to calculate these.

Currently, the advisory fee structure for the FMI Large Cap Equity Composite portfolios is as follows:

Up to $25,000,000 0.55%

$50,000,001-$100,000,000 0.45%

The firm generally requires a minimum of $3 million in assets to establish a discretionary account. High Net Worth individuals may establish an account with a minimum of $1,000,000, however, the firm reserves the right to charge a minimum dollar fee for High Net Worth individuals depending on the client servicing involved. The minimum account sizes do not apply to new accounts for which there is a corporate, family, or other substantial relationship to existing accounts. In addition, the firm reserves the right to waive the minimum account size and minimum annual fee under certain circumstances. A complete list and description of all firm composites and FMI distributed mutual funds are available upon request.

The S&P 500 Index® is widely regarded as the best single gauge of the U.S. equities market. This index includes 500 leading companies in leading industries of the U.S. economy. Although the S&P 500® focuses on the large cap segment of the market, with approximately 75% coverage of U.S. equities, it is also an ideal proxy for the total market. The Large Cap Equity composite uses the S&P 500 Index® as its primary index comparison.
